title: The Lay Folks’ Catechism: 1901 parallel-text collection
summary: CME00041 is the repository XML representation of the title matter and printed pp. 1–99 of Thomas Frederick Simmons and Henry Edward Nolloth’s 1901 EETS parallel-text volume. That source coordinates four medieval documents: the Middle English instruction commissioned by Archbishop John Thoresby from John de Gaitrik (T), Thoresby’s Latin instruction (C), an eclectic Middle English catechetical compilation copied in Lambeth Palace Library MS 408 and historically but misleadingly called a Wycliffite adaptation (L), and John Peckham’s Latin Ignorancia sacerdotum canons (P). The edition transcribes T and C from Borthwick Institute Abp Reg 11, L principally from Lambeth MS 408 with bracketed additions from York Minster MS XVI.L.12, and does not identify the copy used for P. A complete 198-page scan of the 1901 volume and the complete 893-image object exposed for Abp Reg 11 are cached. The register bundle retains boards, pastedowns, guards, flyleaves, binding fragments, all exposed numbered folios, extra gutter views, and the color reference; the corpus passages are mapped within it to sequence positions 655–662, ff. 295r–298v.
review_notes: The graph treats the four coordinated medieval documents, physical witnesses, 1901 edition, U-M encoding, repository XML, and digital facsimiles as distinct layers.

The 1901 phrase ‘Wycliffite adaptation’ is preserved as attribution history only. Modern research describes Lambeth MS 408, ff. 1r–17r as an eclectic catechetical compilation and rejects both Wycliffite authorship and its treatment as a clean reworking of the Lay Folks’ Catechism.
Similarity between Peckham’s 1281 pastoral canons and Thoresby’s 1357 program does not establish direct textual derivation; no copied_from relation is asserted between P and T or C.

The complete Abp Reg 11 cache is complete relative to the provider’s 893 published image resources, not a claim to preservation-master completeness. The cached JPEGs are full-color 1800-pixel-wide access derivatives.

The official Abp Reg 11 Presentation 2 document places Canvas objects directly in sequences rather than inside a conventional Sequence.canvases array. The bundle therefore inventories every official image resource without silently repairing the published manifest.
A search of the existing source cache found no exact Abp Reg 11 bundle suitable for hardlink reuse before download.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00041
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository’s conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID are all CME00041.
TEMPHEAD records ‘8-4-16’; this ambiguous inherited review notation is not normalized as a production or revision date.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00041
entities.description: Public manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from selected matter in the 1901 EETS edition. It includes edition title matter, the printed text overview, and the four serialized text streams through printed p. 99; it omits the introduction, notes, glossary, and index.

entities.label: Simmons and Nolloth, The Lay Folks’ Catechism (1901)
entities.description: EETS parallel-text edition coordinating the English and Latin Thoresby texts, the Lambeth catechetical compilation with York additions, and Peckham’s Lambeth canons. Simmons prepared the texts, notes, and draft reference matter; Nolloth completed and revised the volume and wrote its introduction.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Frederick Simmons and Henry Edward Nolloth, eds., The Lay Folks’ Catechism; or, The English and Latin Versions of Archbishop Thoresby’s Instruction for the People; Together with a Wycliffite Adaptation of the Same, and the Corresponding Canons of the Council of Lambeth, EETS Original Series 118 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, 1901), xxxix + 137 pp.
bibliographic.title: The Lay Folks’ Catechism; or, The English and Latin Versions of Archbishop Thoresby’s Instruction for the People; Together with a Wycliffite Adaptation of the Same, and the Corresponding Canons of the Council of Lambeth
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 118
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

physical_description.extent: xxxix preliminary pages and 137 Arabic-numbered pages
notes: CME00041 represents the title matter and printed pp. 1–99; the cached scan retains the complete volume, including the introduction and pp. 101–137 of notes, glossary, and index.

entities.label: Borthwick Institute for Archives, Abp Reg 11
entities.description: Archiepiscopal register containing the English instruction at ff. 295r–297r and the Latin instruction at ff. 297v–298v. Modern scholarship identifies Thomas de Aldefeld as scribe and describes the leaves as an independent quaternion, possibly an official exemplar before binding into the register.

physical_description.extent: 400 numbered folios plus binding matter and flyleaves exposed in the public image sequence
notes: The corpus passages occupy ff. 295r–298v, public image positions 655–662.
The complete public sequence additionally exposes boards, pastedowns, guards, flyleaves, binding fragments, extra f. 56r gutter views, and a final color-reference image.

entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library, MS 408
entities.description: Composite codex whose fifteenth-century first booklet contains at ff. 1r–17r the eclectic catechetical compilation printed as text L. Modern research separates its Pater Noster, Ave Maria, Creed, Ten Commandments, and related compatible materials and rejects the 1901 theory that the compilation is a Wycliffite adaptation of Thoresby’s instruction.

physical_description.extent: 129 principal physical folios with complex slipped inserts; Booklet I ff. 1r–17r contains the text represented here
physical_description.format: Composite codex bound in calf over wooden boards
notes: Earlier descriptions counted 141 leaves by treating foliated slips separately.
The first booklet’s language has Norfolk, Ely, and Lincolnshire associations; this is witness language, not secure evidence for the compilation’s place of composition.

entities.label: York Minster Library, MS XVI.L.12
entities.description: Medieval manuscript from which the 1901 editors supplied bracketed passages within text L. Modern research says it contains similar catechetical material but is not itself a witness of the Lay Folks’ Catechism.

holding.notes: Only about one-third of York Minster collection descriptions are online; no exact stable public child record for XVI.L.12 was established.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of the 1901 Lay Folks’ Catechism
entities.description: A 198-page full-volume scan of the University of Toronto copy. It retains covers and copy-specific matter, title and preliminary matter, the complete edited text, notes, glossary, and index.

entities.label: University of York complete public image object for Abp Reg 11
entities.description: The entire 893-image object exposed by the Archbishops’ Registers Revealed site, cached from an exact official image-URL inventory at 1800-pixel width. It includes all exposed binding matter and numbered leaves, not only the eight images containing CME00041’s T and C texts.
entities.reproduction_format: 893 full-color sRGB JPEG access derivatives, each requested with IIIF width syntax 1800,

entities.label: Ian Cornelius, ‘The Lay Folks’ Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus’ (2019)
entities.description: Peer-reviewed study of the English catechism’s commission, witness tradition, form, and rhetorical rhythm. It identifies twenty-six witnesses, treats Abp Reg 11 and Bodleian Don.c.13 as the two best, and argues that the English text is a deliberately plain, category-defying form rather than confidently identifiable traditional alliterative verse.

bibliographic.citation: Ian Cornelius, ‘The Lay Folks’ Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus’, Review of English Studies 70 (2019): 14–36, doi:10.1093/res/hgy107.
bibliographic.title: The Lay Folks’ Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus

entities.label: Pamela L. C. Greig, The ‘Lay Folks’ Catechism’—An Edition (2018)
entities.description: Doctoral thesis and modern critical edition using Bodleian Don.c.13 as base and variants from twenty-six witnesses. It re-evaluates the 1901 edition, rejects the Wycliffite-adaptation theory for Lambeth MS 408, explains that York Minster XVI.L.12 is not a Lay Folks’ Catechism witness, and cautions against treating Peckham’s canons as a proven direct source.

bibliographic.citation: Pamela L. C. Greig, The ‘Lay Folks’ Catechism’—An Edition (PhD thesis, University of Nottingham, 2018).
bibliographic.title: The ‘Lay Folks’ Catechism’—An Edition

access.access_method: Consult by appointment under Lambeth Palace Library reading-room procedures.
Submit the official digital-photography order form; publication requires separate permission.
access.format: High-quality TIFF/JPEG or standard PDF from microfilm
notes: The posted form quotes £14.40 including VAT per high-quality TIFF/JPEG and £8.40 for the first standard microfilm PDF item plus £1.20 for subsequent items, with a 40-scan order limit and up to four weeks’ turnaround.

A dated search of the official LUNA image collection and indexed public records found no complete exposed MS 408 digital object. This is a negative discovery claim, not proof that no internal digitization exists.

The collection page states that only about one-third of descriptions are online; the catalog returned an upstream error during the dated check and no stable exact MS XVI.L.12 child record was established.

access.access_method: Arrange an appointment with the Collections team for reading-room consultation.
Ask the Collections team for case-by-case copying, reproduction, and publication permission.

notes: No full public facsimile or IIIF object for MS XVI.L.12 was found in the dated search. The site’s selectively illustrated Torre survey does not establish image access for this manuscript.
The thesis is publicly readable in a browser. Automated direct retrieval was blocked by the repository’s web-application firewall with HTTP 405/CAPTCHA, so no local copy is asserted.

rights.basis: No file-specific license was established during this review; rights in the medieval content and in modern encoding/markup are separate.
The U-M item labels the resource public domain and applies No Copyright–United States; its educational/research access notice remains provider guidance.
Published in 1901; Internet Archive metadata marks the scanned volume NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and reports no visible copyright notice.
The item’s metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT and no visible copyright notice, but no explicit reusable-file license was found for the scan itself.
The texts were composed and copied in the fourteenth century. This conclusion does not apply to modern photographs or metadata.

rights.component: Published Abp Reg 11 manuscript images and IIIF object
rights.basis: The exact official manifest states CC BY-SA 4.0 and attribution ‘Made available by the University of York.’
The manuscript content is medieval; modern catalog descriptions and reproductions remain separate rights components.

rights.component: New reproductions supplied by Lambeth Palace Library
rights.basis: The order form does not grant publication rights; the separate permission form limits approved use, requires acknowledgement, can charge a fee, and provides nonexclusive permission for a specific use and period.
The content is medieval; this does not establish rights in a modern catalog record or reproduction.

rights.component: Copies and images supplied by York Minster
rights.basis: The access policy handles reproduction case by case and requires specific application and consent for commercial, publication, or website use.
The cached author manuscript states © The Author 2018 and Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 3.0.

rights.component: Greig thesis PDF and modern critical edition
rights.basis: The repository copy states All Rights Reserved and provides no open-content license.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A complete scholarly volume with introduction, edited parallel texts, notes, glossary, and index
editorial_practices.representation: Edition title matter and printed pp. 1–99 only
editorial_practices.description: The electronic text selects the title matter, one-page text overview, and four edited text streams; it excludes the edition introduction and reference apparatus.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Four texts coordinated by the printed edition’s parallel page layout
editorial_practices.representation: Four source streams serialized as T, C, L, and P divisions
editorial_practices.description: The XML serializes texts that the 1901 print coordinates in upper/lower and even/odd arrangements. Reused printed PB references therefore identify layout coordinates, not 176 different printed pages.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Lambeth MS 408 catechetical compilation with similar material in York Minster MS XVI.L.12
editorial_practices.representation: Lambeth base transcription with bracketed additions from the York manuscript
editorial_practices.description: The edition’s L text is not a transcription of one physical source throughout; bracketed supplied matter must remain associated specifically with York Minster MS XVI.L.12.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The English instruction is punctuated as continuous prose in Abp Reg 11, while other early northern witnesses use lineation
editorial_practices.representation: Verse-like editorial lines in text T
editorial_practices.description: The 1901 edition and XML display the English text as lines. Modern analysis warns that this is not sufficient evidence for traditional alliterative metre and describes the work as a deliberately plain, category-defying form.

evidence.locator: IDG; title matter; BODY/DIV1–DIV2; DIVINFO/TEXTID; PB, MILESTONE, P, LG, L, NOTE
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for repository identity, selected source matter, encoded languages, four serialized medieval text streams, printed and manuscript locators, and XML structure.

evidence.locator: Element and direct-component counts for DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, L, LG, MILESTONE, NOTE, P, and PB
evidence.summary: Confirmed three DIV1, four DIV2, twenty HEAD, 1,550 L, twenty-five LG, 558 MILESTONE, 413 NOTE, 215 P, and 176 PB elements. Direct component counts are T 581 L/39 PB, C 95 P/39 PB, L 969 L/54 P/58 PB, and P 57 P/37 PB; 176 PB elements represent 101 unique printed REF values because the parallel streams reuse printed-page coordinates.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English, CME00041 item and full text
evidence.summary: Official discovery and text evidence for the stable identifier, title, public electronic encoding, and immediate 1901 source edition.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan, CME00041 access and rights notice
evidence.summary: The item identifies the material as public domain and No Copyright–United States and gives educational/research access guidance.

evidence.summary: Primary edition evidence for title, editors, publication, four-text arrangement, witness statements, editorial presentation, target printed pages, and excluded apparatus. The cached PDF is complete at 198 pages.
Evidence for scan provenance and files, public access, item identifier, and the NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT/no-visible-notice rights metadata.

evidence.citation: University of York, IIIF Presentation 2 document for Abp Reg 11

evidence.citation: Local verification of the complete Abp Reg 11 IIIF image-URL bundle
evidence.locator: inventory.json; source-list.json; images/000001.jpg–images/000893.jpg; unzip integrity test; sampled image dimensions at positions 1, 655, 662, and 893

evidence.citation: Ian Cornelius, ‘The Lay Folks’ Catechism, Alliterative Verse, and Cursus’, Review of English Studies 70 (2019): 14–36
evidence.locator: author manuscript, especially commission, witness census, Abp Reg 11 description, lineation, and conclusions on form
evidence.summary: Peer-reviewed evidence for the 1357 commission to John de Gaitrik, twenty-six witnesses, the authority and physical context of Abp Reg 11, and the conclusion that the English text is not securely traditional alliterative verse. The author manuscript states CC BY-NC-ND 3.0.

evidence.citation: Pamela L. C. Greig, The ‘Lay Folks’ Catechism’—An Edition (PhD thesis, University of Nottingham, 2018)
evidence.locator: witness census and descriptions; history of scholarship; evaluation of Simmons and Nolloth; Lambeth 408 and York XVI.L.12; Peckham discussion
evidence.summary: Modern critical evidence for title and name history, 1357 production context, twenty-six-witness census, Abp Reg 11 and Lambeth MS 408 descriptions, York MS use, transcription problems in 1901, rejection of the Wycliffite-adaptation theory, and caution against a direct Peckham-source claim. The repository PDF states All Rights Reserved.

evidence.citation: Lambeth Palace Library Archives catalogue, MS 408
evidence.summary: Official item URL and holding evidence. The page returned HTTP 403 in the research environment; manuscript details are therefore grounded principally in the edition and Greig rather than inferred from inaccessible HTML.

evidence.citation: Lambeth Palace Library, Digital Photography order form and price list
evidence.summary: Official evidence for request route, formats, posted per-image and microfilm-PDF charges, forty-scan order limit, turnaround, and the distinction between supplying a reproduction and permitting publication.

evidence.citation: Lambeth Palace Library, Permission to Reproduce application
evidence.summary: Official evidence that publication permission is separate, nonexclusive, limited to the approved use and period, subject to acknowledgement and possible fees, and not inferred from receipt of copies.

evidence.citation: Dated search of Lambeth Palace Library’s official LUNA image collection and indexed MS 408 records
evidence.summary: No complete public MS 408 image object, IIIF manifest, or exact digitized-item result was found as of 2026-07-12. Reproduction ordering remains available.

evidence.citation: York Minster, Collections, Archive and Library
evidence.summary: Official evidence for holding, catalog coverage, research access, contact route, and the limited scope of currently illustrated online projects.

evidence.citation: York Minster, Collections Access Policy, approved June 2024 and updated 6 June 2025
evidence.summary: Official evidence for appointment-based access, case-by-case copying and fees, and required specific consent for commercial, publication, or website reproduction.

evidence.citation: Dated search for York Minster MS XVI.L.12 in the Minster catalog and indexed public image resources
evidence.summary: No stable exact catalog child record, public IIIF object, or complete facsimile for MS XVI.L.12 was established as of 2026-07-12; the catalog also returned an upstream error during direct inspection.

open_questions.question: Which manuscript or printed source did Simmons and Nolloth use for text P, Peckham’s Ignorancia sacerdotum canons?
next_steps: Review the complete introduction and notes for any overlooked source siglum.
Consult modern editions and the surviving-manuscript tradition of the Lambeth canons.
Do not infer a base witness or a direct source relation to Thoresby without explicit evidence.

open_questions.question: Which exact folios and passages in York Minster MS XVI.L.12 correspond to every bracketed addition in the 1901 L text?
next_steps: Request the complete manuscript or at least a complete target-booklet reproduction, not cropped passages alone.
Collate the 1901 brackets against the manuscript and record folio-level mappings.
Keep the manuscript classified as a supporting source rather than a Lay Folks’ Catechism witness unless new evidence changes the modern assessment.

open_questions.question: Has Lambeth Palace Library MS 408 been digitized internally, or can a complete whole-manuscript facsimile be ordered despite the posted forty-scan limit?
next_steps: Ask archives@churchofengland.org specifically for the complete manuscript object and an estimate.
If only staged orders or microfilm are possible, preserve the whole-object request and document any unavoidable partial coverage.
Map ff. 1r–17r within any complete supplied object.

open_questions.question: Can York Minster supply or expose a complete whole-manuscript facsimile of MS XVI.L.12?
next_steps: Contact collections@yorkminster.org with the exact shelfmark and request the complete manuscript object, price, image license, and publication conditions.
Ask for an existing digital surrogate before commissioning new photography.
Map the L additions inside the complete object once obtained.

open_questions.question: Can the public Greig thesis PDF be cached reproducibly without bypassing the University of Nottingham repository’s access controls?
next_steps: Retry through a documented repository export or API if one becomes available.
Do not bypass CAPTCHA or claim a local copy until a complete file is retrieved and verified.
